Slide Bullet Points:
â€¢ Welcome to Electrical Fundamentals for ELV Engineers.
â€¢ Core focus on basic electrical parameters and circuits.
â€¢ Roadmap of the 12 slides in this session.

â€¢ Matter consists of atoms containing protons, neutrons, and electrons.
â€¢ Free electrons in copper move easily, creating electrical current.
â€¢ Insulating materials (rubber, PVC) block electron flow.

â€¢ Voltage (V) is the electromotive force driving charge carriers.
â€¢ Current (I) is the rate of charge flow measured in Amperes.
â€¢ Ohm's Law states: Current = Voltage / Resistance (I = V/R).

â€¢ Resistance (R) opposes current flow, converting energy to heat.
â€¢ Joule heating formula (P = IÂ²R) explains power dissipation.
â€¢ Selecting correct resistor wattage prevents thermal failure.

â€¢ Electrical power (P) is the rate of energy consumption in Watts.
â€¢ Watt's Law states: Power = Voltage Ã— Current (P = V Ã— I).
â€¢ Sizing power supplies requires calculating total system wattage.

â€¢ Alternating Current (AC) periodically reverses direction.
â€¢ Direct Current (DC) flows continuously in a single direction.
â€¢ DC ripple represents residual AC voltage on a rectified DC line.

â€¢ Carbon film, metal film, and wirewound resistor profiles.
â€¢ Read four-band and five-band color codes to find ohm values.
â€¢ Understanding tolerance ratings (gold = 5%, silver = 10%).

â€¢ Series connection adds resistances: R_total = R1 + R2 + R3.
â€¢ Parallel connection reduces total resistance: 1/R_total = 1/R1 + 1/R2.
â€¢ Applications in access control loop resistor circuits.

â€¢ In series circuits, current is constant; voltage splits.
â€¢ In parallel circuits, voltage is constant; current splits.
â€¢ Most ELV hardware and cameras are wired in parallel configurations.

â€¢ Staircase wiring uses two single-pole double-throw (SPDT) switches.
â€¢ Allows controlling a single light source from two separate locations.
â€¢ Phase/Line terminal routing standard for staircase safety.

â€¢ Stripping wire insulation without damaging internal copper cores.
â€¢ Using bootlace ferrule terminals to prevent wire fraying inside blocks.
â€¢ Selecting the right crimping tool profiles for different wire gauges.

â€¢ Wire up staircase circuits on a testboard.
â€¢ Measure series and parallel voltages using a digital multimeter.
â€¢ Crimp bootlace ferrules onto multi-strand power cables.

â€¢ Welcome to Earthing & Lightning Protection.
â€¢ Focus on system safety, ground paths, and surge suppression.
â€¢ Overview of the 10 slides in this session.

â€¢ Earthing provides a low-resistance path for fault currents to return to ground.
â€¢ Protects users from electric shock due to insulation failure.
â€¢ Stabilizes voltage levels during transient faults.

â€¢ Understanding TN-S, TN-C, and TT grounding setups.
â€¢ Local copper plate, rod, and chemical gel earthing options.
â€¢ Soil moisture enhancement treatments for dry regions.

â€¢ Ground resistivity depends on soil type, moisture, and salt content.
â€¢ Chemical compounds (bentonite, charcoal) lower resistance.
â€¢ Performing soil resistivity tests before drilling earth pits.

â€¢ Safe earth resistance should ideally be under 5 Ohms.
â€¢ High-sensitivity instrumentation systems require under 1 Ohm.
â€¢ Testing resistance periodically using earth tester meters.

â€¢ Franklin rods, early streamer emission (ESE), and Faraday cages.
â€¢ Down conductors path routing guidelines.
â€¢ Maintaining physical separation from electrical wires.

â€¢ Metal Oxide Varistors (MOVs) clamp transient voltage surges.
â€¢ Gas Discharge Tubes (GDTs) handle high-current lightning discharges.
â€¢ Sizing SPDs based on maximum continuous operating voltage (MCOV).

â€¢ Mounting Type 1 and Type 2 SPDs inside distribution boards.
â€¢ Keeping connection lead lengths under 50cm to minimize impedance.
â€¢ Wiring Earth lines directly to the main grounding busbar.

â€¢ Preventing ground loop noise in analog CCTV networks.
â€¢ Shield grounding protocols for STP Ethernet and RS485 communication cables.
â€¢ Isolated grounding bars for sensitive server racks.

â€¢ Measure earth pit resistance using a three-point fall-of-potential tester.
â€¢ Inspect chemical earthing rods and compound treatments.
â€¢ Install and wire a surge protection module in a test DB panel.
